stories.headline: Parent group sues Denver Public Schools, alleges district defrauded voters with bond measures
stories.summary:
A parent organization called Mamás de DPS filed a lawsuit Tuesday in Denver District Court accusing Denver Public Schools of racketeering and violating the Colorado Organized Crime Control Act. The group alleges the district misled voters about the purpose of bond measures, including the nearly $1 billion bond passed two years ago, and used those proceeds to pay off non-voter-approved debt.

At the center of the allegations is a financial arrangement involving a nonprofit called Denver School Facilities Leasing Corporation, which holds titles and mortgages on 33 school buildings and leases them back to DPS. The group's attorney, Lisi Owen, says the district is using bond money to repay certificate-of-participation debt while telling voters the funds are for building maintenance and upgrades.

DPS spokesman Scott Pribble dismissed the suit as 'frivolous,' saying the financial practices challenged by the lawsuit are standard and widely accepted among Colorado school districts and have been repeatedly upheld by state courts. The district said it looks forward to defending itself.

The lawsuit is the second filed by Mamás de DPS against the district. The group's first suit, filed in 2024 to halt school closures tied to falling enrollment, remains ongoing. Attorneys said the new filing was prompted by what they described as disappointing misrepresentations by DPS officials in the initial case.

stories.headline: Culinary Creative Group CEO Juan Padro steps down; service charge lawsuit heads toward trial
stories.summary:
Juan Padro, co-founder of Culinary Creative Group — one of Denver's most prominent restaurant groups — is stepping down as chief executive officer after more than 15 years leading the company. Richard Flaherty, former CEO of Punch Bowl Social, will take over leadership as the company pursues national expansion while preserving its chef-driven identity.

Padro helped build Culinary Creative Group into a portfolio of more than a dozen concepts including Señor Bear, Mister Oso, A5 Steakhouse, Tap & Burger, and Ash'Kara. He will remain with the company as a consultant and equity holder. Two other promotions were announced alongside the leadership change: Blake Edmunds becomes chief culinary director, and Lauren Barash joins as the group's first vice president of marketing.

The transition comes as a lawsuit filed by a former Kumoya server, Marianna White, continues in Denver County District Court. White originally alleged the company was diverting employee tips by classifying them as service charges; she later amended the complaint to focus on how the company distinguishes between fixed fees and voluntary customer gratuities.

Denver District Court Judge Sarah Block Wallace declined to pause the case this week, saying she would still schedule a trial date and address the service-charge definition question at an upcoming case management conference. The outcome could have broader implications for Denver restaurants that use service charge models.

stories.headline: Colorado's congressional delegation splits sharply along party lines over Trump State of the Union address
stories.summary:
Colorado's 10-member congressional delegation reacted in starkly divided fashion to President Donald Trump's State of the Union address Tuesday, with the state's four House Republicans praising the speech and its six Democrats denouncing it as misleading and divisive. The reactions came through floor statements, press releases, and a stream of social media posts before and during the nearly two-hour address.

Republican Reps. Gabe Evans, Jeff Crank, Lauren Boebert, and Jeff Hurd each credited Trump with strengthening border security, boosting energy production, and restoring fiscal discipline. Hurd, who had previously lost Trump's endorsement over tariff disagreements, nonetheless said the speech was a moment to 'reflect on progress and focus on the work still ahead.'

Democratic U.S. Sen. John Hickenlooper accused Trump of asking Americans to ignore economic hardship caused by tariffs and what he called 'lawless' ICE operations. Rep. Jason Crow of Aurora, who had been targeted in a since-dropped DOJ indictment effort, attended the address alongside colleagues who appeared in a viral video about the obligation to refuse illegal orders, posting that Trump 'tried to imprison us' but failed.

Rep. Diana DeGette of Denver was the only member of the delegation to skip the address entirely, saying she would not 'legitimize' what she characterized as egregious conduct by the administration. The partisan divide underscores Colorado's role as a politically mixed state with representation on both sides of the deepening national rift over the Trump agenda.

stories.headline: Nuggets hold Celtics to season-low 84 points in 103-84 victory, coach Adelman credits defensive effort
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets snapped a stretch of poor play against winning teams Wednesday night, defeating the Boston Celtics 103-84 at Ball Arena. The 84 points allowed was a season low for Denver and marked only the third time in 59 games the Nuggets have held an opponent below 100 points.

Head coach David Adelman credited an aggressive defensive effort across all four quarters, noting the team has reached its current win total almost entirely on the strength of its offense. He said the performance showed what Denver is capable of if defense becomes more consistent heading into the postseason.

'If we're going to do something uniquely special this season, our defense has to come along,' Adelman said after the game. 'Tonight was a good example of it.' The Nuggets held Celtics All-Star Jaylen Brown in check and limited Boston's perimeter shooters throughout the contest.

Denver had lost six of its previous nine games and seven straight against teams with winning records before Wednesday's result. With injured forwards Aaron Gordon and Peyton Watson expected to return soon, Adelman said the challenge is sustaining that defensive identity regardless of who is in the lineup. The Nuggets face Oklahoma City next.

stories.headline: Update: Douglas County wildfire that burned 1,000-plus acres near Franktown was started by trash truck
stories.summary:
The Douglas County Sheriff's Office has determined that Tuesday's Dahlberg fire, which scorched more than 1,081 acres of dry grass south of Franktown, was ignited when a trash truck dropped a 'hot load' at an unspecified location in the area. The sheriff's office did not specify the circumstances that led the driver to offload the material.

Dry vegetation and winds of up to 20 mph allowed the fire to spread rapidly after it was first reported near Dahlberg and Lake Gulch roads at 12:39 p.m. Tuesday, approximately eight miles south of Franktown and eight miles east of Larkspur. More than a dozen homes and Cherry Valley Elementary School were evacuated, but no property damage was reported and residents were allowed to return the same day.

The fire was declared fully contained Tuesday evening. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor hotspots and guard against rekindling in continued high winds. The cause determination adds context to one of the largest single-day grassfires in the county this winter.

The incident comes amid a period of heightened fire danger across the Front Range, driven by a historically dry winter and snowpack that sits at 63 percent of normal — the worst deficit since 1987.

stories.headline: Jefferson County house fire sparks small wildfire in Golden Gate Canyon, prompts pre-evacuation warnings
stories.summary:
A house fire in western Jefferson County ignited a small wildfire Thursday morning, prompting pre-evacuation warnings across a wide swath of the foothills west of Denver. The fire, dubbed the Geneva fire, was first reported at 11:35 a.m. in the 10600 block of Ralston Creek Road after flames spread from a structure to nearby trees and grass.

Jefferson and Gilpin county officials issued pre-evacuation warnings for a three-mile radius around the blaze, covering Golden Gate Estates, Braecher Ranchettes, and surrounding communities. Colorado Parks and Wildlife also briefly evacuated the southern portion of Golden Gate Canyon State Park and its visitor center as a precaution.

Fire crews stopped forward progress on the blaze by 12:34 p.m. — less than an hour after it was reported — and officials lifted pre-evacuation warnings just after 1 p.m. The fire was declared fully contained Thursday afternoon, having burned less than an acre.

The incident is the latest in a series of grass and brush fires in the Denver metro foothills this winter, driven by below-normal snowpack and dry vegetation across the region.

stories.headline: Colorado plug-in solar bill advances in legislature, could open market for balcony and portable panels by year's end
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are promoting House Bill 1007, a measure that would remove regulatory barriers to so-called plug-in or balcony solar panels — portable photovoltaic units that connect directly to home electrical outlets or battery storage systems without requiring utility approval. Sponsors demonstrated a prototype system at the Capitol rotunda Wednesday, drawing comparisons to Germany, where more than 1 million such units have been installed.

The bill would prohibit retail and wholesale electric providers from imposing unreasonable conditions on consumers who want to install the devices, and would not require customers to seek prior authorization before plugging in a panel. A second provision would ease installation of 'meter collars,' which allow a safe two-way grid connection through existing home electric meters.

Namaste Solar co-owner Jason Sharpe said a plug-in system with battery storage is expected to retail for approximately $2,000 once the Colorado market opens, making it accessible to homeowners who cannot afford full rooftop installations and apartment dwellers previously excluded from solar savings. He noted roughly 30 states are currently advancing similar legislation.

The bill is sponsored by Reps. Lesley Smith of Boulder and Rebekah Stewart of Lakewood-Edgewater. Supporters say the timing is favorable given rising tariff costs and the rollback of federal tax credits for larger solar systems under the current administration.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship to support and retain Black male educators in Denver and Aurora schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, nicknamed 'The Den,' a new program designed to provide professional development, networking, and community support for Black male teachers working in Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools. The fellowship is a collaboration between MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER program, the Office of Education Solutions, both school districts, and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

Program director Dr. Rashad Anderson said the fellowship addresses the profound isolation many Black male educators face in Colorado schools, where they represent a small fraction of the teaching workforce. According to the National Association of State Boards of Education, 40 percent of Colorado school districts have no teacher of color, and 62 percent employ no Black male teachers at all.

Anderson said Black male teachers are frequently expected to serve as unofficial disciplinarians or counselors for all students of color in their buildings, a burden that contributes to burnout and attrition. The fellowship aims to build a sense of brotherhood and shared professional identity to help retain teachers who might otherwise leave the classroom.

The program also seeks to recruit future educators like Kaheem Heath, a current Call Me MiSTER participant who said he never had a Black male teacher during his own schooling. Heath said the experience motivated him to pursue teaching and to inspire other Black men to enter the profession.

stories.headline: Colorado troopers and four metro agencies launch crackdown on dangerous driving along I-25 south of Denver
stories.summary:
Colorado State Patrol joined forces with the Greenwood Village Police Department, Lone Tree Police, the Douglas County Sheriff's Office, and the Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office Wednesday to conduct a concentrated traffic enforcement operation on a 15-mile stretch of Interstate 25 between Castle Rock and Monument. The corridor recorded more than 100 crashes and three traffic fatalities in 2025.

The operation focused on speeding — the third leading cause of traffic crashes in Colorado — as well as compliance with the state's Move Over law, which requires drivers to change lanes for emergency responders, tow trucks, and maintenance vehicles stopped on the shoulder. CSP issued 42,300 speeding citations statewide in 2025.

Preliminary CDOT data shows Colorado recorded 701 traffic deaths in 2025, a 2 percent increase over 2024. Officials attributed part of the year-end surge to an unusually warm November and December that brought more vehicles onto highways during months that typically see reduced travel.

CSP Sgt. Ivan Alvarado said the enforcement effort is not about revenue but about preventing fatalities. 'We don't care about that,' he said. 'What we care about is those 100-plus crashes, those three families that had to hear from us.' The agencies plan continued high-visibility patrols in the corridor.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
